content:
# Waste Category Reference Guide

**Tool ID:** T181-02
**Block:** B181 (Waste Log & Reconciliation)
**Domain:** Operations & Workflow (D05_operations_workflow)
**Level:** L1
**Format:** Reference
**Role:** All Staff
**Time Required:** 2–3 minutes (reference as needed)
**Version:** 1.0

---
## Welcome

When someone throws away a burnt milk jug, is that "preparation waste" or "damaged"? When a customer sends back a latte because it is lukewarm, is that a "return" or a "preparation error"? This guide answers those questions so every team member categorises waste the same way, every time. Consistent categories produce reliable data. Reliable data drives smart decisions.

Pin this next to the waste log. Reference it until the categories are second nature.

**Built for All Staff working in Operations & Workflow.**
Use it. Adapt it. Make it yours.

---

## Purpose

A quick-reference guide defining each waste category used in the Daily Waste Log Sheet (T181-01). Provides clear definitions, examples, and edge-case guidance so that waste is categorised consistently across all staff and shifts.

This tool supports **Waste Log & Reconciliation** (B181) and is designed to be practical, printable, and immediately useful in a working cafe environment.

---

## How to Use This Tool

**Who:** All Staff
**Time Required:** 2–3 minutes to read; then reference as needed when logging waste
**Frequency:** Read once during onboarding. Reference whenever unsure about a category.

**What You'll Need:**
- This guide (printed and posted at the waste logging station, next to T181-01)

**Steps:**
1. Read through all categories and examples during your first shift
2. When logging waste, check the category code against this guide if unsure
3. If an item could fit two categories, use the one that identifies the root cause
4. Ask the shift lead if genuinely unsure — consistency matters more than speed

---

## Reference

### Category P — Preparation / Production Waste

**Definition:** Waste generated during the process of making products, including training, dial-in, recipe errors, and skill-related discards.

| Example | Why It Is Category P |
|---------|---------------------|
| Over-extracted espresso shots (bitter, too slow) | Grind setting or technique error during production |
| Under-extracted shots (sour, too fast) | Grind setting or technique error |
| Burnt/scorched milk (heated above 70 C) | Steaming technique error |
| Morning dial-in shots (2–4 shots to set grind) | Necessary production calibration waste |
| Latte art practice shots (using real milk) | Training-related production waste |
| Over-portioned food (extra avocado, too much cheese) | Portion control error |
| Failed recipe attempt (wrong ingredients, wrong method) | Production error |
| Batch brew that tastes off (wrong ratio, stale) | Brewing parameter error |

**Edge cases:**
- Customer sends back a drink because it is poorly made (wrong temperature, bad taste) = **P** (root cause is preparation quality)
- Milk steamed but not used because order was cancelled = **R** (root cause is the cancelled order, not the steaming)

### Category E — Expired / Spoiled

**Definition:** Items discarded because they have passed their use-by date, turned, spoiled, or are no longer safe to serve. Includes items that deteriorated faster than expected.

| Example | Why It Is Category E |
|---------|---------------------|
| Milk past use-by date | Time-based expiry |
| Milk that has turned sour before use-by date | Premature spoilage (check fridge temperature) |
| Avocados gone brown/overripe | Produce deterioration |
| Salad leaves wilted beyond service standard | Produce deterioration |
| Sandwich filling past labelled use-by | Time-based expiry on prepped food |
| Cream that has split | Spoilage |
| Eggs past best-before date | Time-based expiry |

**Edge cases:**
- Food prepped yesterday that was not labelled with a date and is now questionable = **E** (it is expired/spoiled; but also flag the labelling failure)
- Fresh produce delivered already in poor condition = **E** (but also raise with supplier)

### Category D — Damaged / Dropped / Contaminated

**Definition:** Items discarded due to physical damage, accidental dropping, spillage, or contamination. The item was fine before the incident.

| Example | Why It Is Category D |
|---------|---------------------|
| Croissant dropped on the floor | Physical damage — cannot serve |
| Coffee spilled before serving | Accidental loss |
| Sandwich fell apart during assembly | Physical damage during handling |
| Plate broken with food on it | Food contaminated by breakage |
| Item cross-contaminated (allergen contact) | Contamination — food safety |
| Packaging torn, contents exposed | Packaging failure |
| Drink knocked over by staff or customer before consumption | Accidental spillage |

**Edge cases:**
- Customer drops their own drink = **D** (damaged, but note "customer" in notes — may warrant a free replacement)
- Delivery driver drops the bag = log only if you witness it and replace the order

### Category O — Over-production / Over-ordering

**Definition:** Items discarded because too much was made or ordered relative to demand. The product was fine — there was simply too much of it.

| Example | Why It Is Category O |
|---------|---------------------|
| Batch brew remaining at end of its 2-hour window | Brewed too much for demand |
| Sandwiches prepped beyond what was sold | Over-estimated demand |
| Soup made in excess of daily sales | Over-production |
| Stock delivered in excess of par level and now expiring | Over-ordering |
| Salad prepped for lunch rush but rush was quiet | Demand forecasting error |

**Edge cases:**
- Over-production that is then kept and expires the next day = **E** (at the point of discard, it is expired; but the root cause was over-production — note this)
- Batch brew poured down the drain at exactly the 2-hour mark = **O** (over-production, even though the time limit is correct)

### Category R — Returns / Remakes

**Definition:** Items discarded because of a customer interaction — wrong order, complaint, change of mind, or allergy concern raised after preparation.

| Example | Why It Is Category R |
|---------|---------------------|
| Flat white made with dairy but customer wanted oat | Wrong order — requires remake |
| Food returned because customer changed their mind | Customer-initiated discard |
| Drink remade because customer said it was cold | Customer complaint — even if drink was correct |
| Order cancelled after preparation started | Cancelled order |
| Item made but customer left before pickup | Abandoned order |

**Edge cases:**
- Drink returned because it was genuinely badly made = **P** (root cause is preparation, not the customer)
- Customer claims allergy after consuming half the item = **R** (customer-initiated return)

### Category U — Unsold End-of-Day

**Definition:** Items discarded at close of business because they were not sold during the trading day. These items were properly made and stored but simply not purchased.

| Example | Why It Is Category U |
|---------|---------------------|
| Display pastries remaining at closing | Not sold during service |
| Prepped sandwiches/wraps not sold | Not sold during service |
| Cake slices remaining in display at closing | Not sold during service |
| Grab-and-go items unsold | Not sold during service |

**Edge cases:**
- Items donated to charity at end of day = still log as **U** but add "donated" in notes (waste avoided but production cost still incurred)
- Items reduced in price and sold = NOT waste — do not log (it was sold)
- Items moved to staff meals = still log as **U** but note "staff meal" (the business absorbed the cost)

### Decision Flowchart

When categorising waste, ask these questions in order:

1. **Was it the end of the trading day?** Yes = **U** (Unsold end-of-day)
2. **Did a customer trigger the discard?** Yes = **R** (Return/remake)
3. **Was it physically damaged, dropped, or contaminated?** Yes = **D** (Damaged)
4. **Had it expired or spoiled?** Yes = **E** (Expired)
5. **Was too much made or ordered?** Yes = **O** (Over-production)
6. **Was it a preparation or production error?** Yes = **P** (Preparation)

---

## Common Risks

### Risk 1: Inconsistent Categorisation Across Staff

**Why it happens:** Different team members interpret categories differently. One person logs a wrong-order remake as P, another logs it as R.
**How to prevent:** Post this guide next to the waste log. During onboarding, walk new staff through 5 example scenarios and ask them to categorise. Correct immediately.
**If it's already happening:** Run a 5-minute team exercise: describe 5 waste scenarios, have everyone write down the category code. Discuss any differences.

### Risk 2: Defaulting to "Other" or Skipping the Category

**Why it happens:** Staff are unsure which category fits, so they leave it blank or write "misc."
**How to prevent:** There is no "other" category on purpose. Every waste item fits one of the six categories. If genuinely unsure, use the decision flowchart above. If still unsure, ask the shift lead.
**If it's already happening:** Review last week's logs. Re-categorise any blank or miscellaneous entries using this guide.

### Risk 3: Arguing About Categories Instead of Logging

**Why it happens:** Two staff members disagree about whether a returned drink is P or R. The debate takes longer than the logging.
**How to prevent:** The rule is: pick the category that identifies the root cause. If unsure, pick the first match in the decision flowchart and move on. Consistency over precision.
**If it's already happening:** The shift lead has final say on category disputes. Do not let categorisation debates delay waste logging.

---

## Customisation Notes

**This tool is a starting point, not a rulebook.**

**Independent / Specialty Cafe:** Consider adding a sub-category for "training waste" under P to separate skill development from actual errors.
**Chain / Franchise:** Map these categories to your corporate waste reporting codes. Most chains use similar categories but different labels.
**Kiosk / Mobile:** Categories D and U will be your most common. Focus training on these two.
**Bakery Cafe:** Category U will dominate. Consider splitting into "donated," "staff meal," and "binned" sub-categories for better analysis.

**Making it yours:**
- Print this guide in A4, laminate it, and pin it directly next to the waste log clipboard
- Highlight the edge cases that are most common in your venue
- Add venue-specific examples that match your menu items
- Review and update examples when your menu changes

The structure matters more than the specific fields. Keep the structure, change the content.

---

## Connected Resources

**Other tools in this block:**

- T181-03 — Weekly Waste Reconciliation Report (L2, template)
- T181-04 — Waste Reconciliation SOP (L2, SOP)
- T181-05 — Monthly Waste Trend Dashboard (L3, dashboard)

**Related blocks:**

**Parent block:** B181 — Waste Log & Reconciliation
**Module:** M14: Operational Rhythm & Daily Discipline
---

*Tool Version 1.0 — OIS Cafe v2*
*Built for cafe operators who value clarity, consistency, and craft.*
